perturbation.original_text: 5. All Confidential Information in any form and any medium, including all copies thereof, disclosed to the Recipient shall be returned to UNHCR or destroyed: (a) if a business relationship is not entered into with UNHCR on or before the date which is three (3) months after the date both Parties have signed the Agreement; or (b) promptly upon request by the UNHCR at any time.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. All Confidential Information in any form and any medium, including all copies thereof, disclosed to the Recipient shall be returned to UNHCR or destroyed: (a) if a business relationship is not entered into with UNHCR on or before the date which is one (1) month after the date both Parties have signed the Agreement; or (b) promptly upon request by the UNHCR at any time.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the deadline for returning or destroying confidential information if a business relationship isn't established from three months to one month could be problematic if it doesn't allow sufficient time for the bidder to fairly assess the information and prepare a response, potentially conflicting with fair competition laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: United States antitrust laws and fair competition regulations may be implicated if the reduced timeframe hinders the bidder's ability to compete fairly.
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the timeframe to one month may not provide sufficient opportunity for a fair assessment of the Confidential Information for bid preparation, potentially hindering competition in violation of antitrust principles. This is because bid preparation can be complex and require significant time for analysis.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 1 / § 1 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 1 : Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 1-MONOPOLIES AND COMBINATIONS IN RESTRAINT OF TRADE Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date Short Title Executive Documents §1. Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the timeframe for assessing confidential information, potentially hindering fair competition. This aligns with the Perturbation and Law Explanations, which suggest a conflict with antitrust principles due to insufficient time for bid preparation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 1, declares illegal any 'contract, combination...or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce.' While the snippet doesn't explicitly define 'restraint of trade,' the reduced timeframe could be interpreted as creating such a restraint if it unfairly disadvantages potential bidders, making it difficult to assess the confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Any dispute, controversy or claim between the Parties arising out of, this Agreement or the breach, termination or invalidity thereof, unless settled amicab ly within twenty (20) days after receipt by one Party of the other Party's request for such amicable settlement, shall be referred by either Party to arbitration in accordance with the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules then obtaining, including provisions on appl icable law. The arbitral tribunal shall have no authority to award punitive damages . In addition, unless otherwise expressly provided in this Agreement, the arbitral tribunal shall have no authority to award interest. The Parties shall be bound by any ar bitration award rendered as a result of such arbitration as the final adjudication of any such controversy, claim or dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Any dispute, controversy or claim between the Parties arising out of, this Agreement or the breach, termination or invalidity thereof, unless settled amicab ly within five (5) days after receipt by one Party of the other Party's request for such amicable settlement, shall be referred by either Party to arbitration in accordance with the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules then obtaining, including provisions on appl icable law. The arbitral tribunal shall have no authority to award punitive damages . In addition, unless otherwise expressly provided in this Agreement, the arbitral tribunal shall have no authority to award interest. The Parties shall be bound by any ar bitration award rendered as a result of such arbitration as the final adjudication of any such controversy, claim or dispute.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the amicable settlement period from 20 to 5 days may be considered unreasonably short, potentially conflicting with laws or principles requiring a fair opportunity to resolve disputes before resorting to arbitration. Some jurisdictions could view this as procedurally unconscionable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of procedural fairness and unconscionability in contract law, specifically regarding dispute resolution processes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification reduces the time for amicable settlement to 5 days, which may be deemed unconscionably short, preventing a fair opportunity to resolve the dispute before arbitration. This could violate the principle of unconscionability as described in UCC § 2-302, particularly if one party has significantly more bargaining power.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

Stronger States. Skip main navigation (Press Enter). Home Better Laws Since 1892, the ULC has provided states with non-partisan, carefully conceived uniform laws. Our work simplifies life for people who live, work, or travel in multiple states and improves local economies by facilitating interstate commerce. Each uniform act is drafted in an open and deliberative process that draws on the expertise of state-appointed commissioners, legal advisors and observers. Learn More About Us Stronger States The ULC promotes state autonomy by providing a process for state governments to collaborate on issues where uniformity of law is necessary but federal oversight is not. Uniform acts provide rules and procedures that are consistent from state to state but that also reflect the diverse experience of the states when appropriate. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the amicable settlement period from 20 to 5 days. The Law Explanation states this may be deemed unconscionably short, preventing a fair opportunity to resolve the dispute before arbitration, potentially violating the principle of unconscionability. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-302, states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. Reducing the settlement period to 5 days creates an inconsistency by making it unlikely that a party would be able to adequately prepare to negotiate, which could be deemed unconscionable.

perturbation.original_text: 8. This Agreement shall enter into force on the date it is signed by bo th Parties. Either Party may terminate the working relationship contemplated by this Agreement by providing written notice to the other, provided, however, that the obligations and restrictions hereunder regarding the Confidential Information shall remain effective following any such termination or any other termination or expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. This Agreement shall enter into force on the date it is signed by bo th Parties. Either Party may terminate the working relationship contemplated by this Agreement with no written notice to the other, provided, however, that the obligations and restrictions hereunder regarding the Confidential Information shall remain effective following any such termination or any other termination or expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for written notice before terminating the agreement could conflict with legal principles requiring good faith and fair dealing in contractual relationships.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, and potential violation of contract law principles related to reasonable notice of termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirement of written notice for termination, the changed text potentially violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as it allows for abrupt termination without affording the other party an opportunity to mitigate damages or make alternative arrangements. Section 205 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts implies that every contract imposes a duty of good faith and fair dealing in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for written notice before terminating the agreement. The Law Explanation states this potentially violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, because it allows for abrupt termination without affording the other party an opportunity to mitigate damages or make alternative arrangements. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines good faith as encompassing honest dealing and observance of fair dealing standards. By removing the written notice, the modified contract undermines the principle of fair dealing, as it permits termination without any consideration for the other party's position, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1. “Confidential Information ”, whenever used in this Agreement , shall mean any data, document, specification and other information or material , that is delivered or disclosed by UNHCR to the Recipient in any form whatsoever, whether orall y, visually in writing or otherwise (including computerized form), and that, at the time of disclosure to the Recipient , is designated as confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. “Confidential Information ”, whenever used in this Agreement , shall mean any data, document, specification and other information or material , that is delivered or disclosed by UNHCR to the Recipient in any form whatsoever, whether orall y, visually in writing or otherwise (including computerized form), and that, at the time of disclosure to the Recipient , is assumed to be confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "designated as confidential" to "assumed to be confidential" weakens the protection of the information and creates ambiguity. In some jurisdictions, this could allow a recipient to argue that information not explicitly marked or stated as confidential was not subject to the agreement's confidentiality provisions, potentially violating trade secret laws if the information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.contradicted_law: State trade secret laws, such as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing "designated as confidential" to "assumed to be confidential", the agreement weakens the requirement for explicit designation, potentially failing to meet the standard of 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' required to protect trade secrets under the UTSA. This could result in the loss of trade secret protection if the discloser does not actively designate the information as confidential.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: trade secret | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you trade secret The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) is a piece of legislation created by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) . The UTSA defines trade secrets and describes claims related to trade secrets. As of 2024, 48 states , the District of Columbia, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and Puerto Rico, have adopted the UTSA. What is a Trade Secret UTSA's Definition The UTSA defines a " trade secret " as: Information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Non-UTSA Definition Prior to the development of the UTSA, improper use or disclosure of a trade secret was traditionally a common law tort . Sections 757 and 758 of the Restatement of Torts (1939) set forth the basic principles of trade secret law that were widely adopted by U.S. courts . In particular, §757, comment b, listed six factors to be considered in determining whether information constitutes a trade secret: The extent to which the information is known outside the claimant's business. The extent to which it is known by employees and others involved in the business. The extent of measures taken by the claimant to guard the secrecy of the information. The value of the information to the business and its competitors. The amount of effort or money expended by the business in developing the information. The ease or difficulty with which the information could be properly acquired or duplicated by others. Elements of a Trade Secret Claim There are typically three essential elements to a trade secret claim: The subject matter involved must qualify for trade secret protection (see "Scope" below for more on this). The holder of the subject matter must establish that reasonable precautions were taken to prevent disclosure of the subject matter. The trade secret holder must prove that the information was misappropriated or wrongfully taken. Use of a trade secret belonging to another does not always constitute misappropriation. There are two basic situations in which obtaining the use of a trade secret is illegal; where it is acquired through improper means, or where it involves a breach of confidence. Trade secrets may be obtained by lawful means such as independent discovery, reverse engineering, and inadvertent disclosure resulting from the trade secret holder's failure to take reasonable protective measures. The misappropriation of trade secrets is considered a form of unfair competition , and is discussed in the Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition . Misappropriation of Trade Secrets as a Crime In some circumstances, misappropriation of trade secrets is not only a tort ; it is a federal crime . Economic Espionage Act In 1996, Congress enacted the Economic Espionage Act ("EEA"), codified as 18 U.S.C. §§1831-1839 . In United States v. Sazonov , the defendant Sazonov has been charged with violating the EEA for "attempt[ing] to steal valuable proprietary computer code that took his employer years to develop . . . Sazonov was employed for thirteen years by Susquehanna International Group ("Susquehanna"), a financial services firm headquartered in Pennsylvania with offices in Manhattan." Scope of Subject Matter Customer Lists Customer lists and other lists related to customer business qualify for trade secret protection if the lists' information cannot be ascertained from other generally available sources. In Morlife Inc. v. Perry, 56 Cal. App. 4th 1514 (1997) , the California Court held that customer identities from an organization's list are protected as trade secrets if the identities are not generally kno
perturbation.justification: The change from "designated as confidential" to "assumed to be confidential" weakens the protection of confidential information. The UTSA, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires "reasonable efforts...to maintain its secrecy." By changing the language, the contract shifts the burden of designating information as confidential, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard and risking trade secret protection. The change introduces ambiguity regarding what information is actually protected, weakening the legal obligation.
